findings.title: FIX-20 CI-separated-min is a no-op: the frozen estimator always returns the raw min over all base×regime cells
findings.detail: _ci_separated_min (lines 520-548) can only ever select eligible[0] (the finest candidate). Trace: it loops candidates finest-first; the finest wins via break iff its boot_ci90 <= min(jnd of the rest); if NOT separated, winner is set to None and the loop continues, but every later candidate c has c.jnd > eligible[0].jnd and c.boot_ci90 >= c.jnd, so c.boot_ci90 <= min(jnd of rest)=eligible[0].jnd is impossible — no later candidate can ever win. After the loop `winner=eligible[0]` (the raw min) with separated=False. I verified this live: an UNSEPARATED finest (jnd=0.05, ci90=0.40) overlapping a tight coarser candidate (jnd=0.20) still returns jnd=0.05, byte-identical to the separated case. So sel['jnd']==raw_min in 100% of cases. This defeats FIX-20 entirely — the winner's-curse correction the protocol budgeted against (raw min over ~3 bases × 6 regimes ≈ 18 noisy tail-quantiles, biased fine by ~1.8σ) is never applied. It also contradicts BOTH the function's own docstring ('resolved to the runner-up ... conservative COARSE-ward') and protocol §4/FIX-20 ('bases whose CIs overlap are pooled'). Because results are write-once (FIX-32) and the JND feeds N in the DSR gate, freezing this locks in an N biased artifactually fine — exactly the 'artifact-driven fineness must be stripped' guard the pre-registration exists to enforce. Phase-A backtests are unaffected (deltas are raw), so re-running Phase B after the fix is cheap — but the buggy selector must not be the one that writes/hashes calibration-v3-results.json.
findings.protocol_ref: §4 FIX-20 (CI-separated-min / pool overlapping); §0 secondary-danger 'strip artifactual fineness'

findings.title: MAX_CRASH_RATE (5% per-dial ceiling, FIX-13) is defined but never enforced or even computable from output
findings.detail: MAX_CRASH_RATE=0.05 (line 132) is referenced nowhere. The Phase-B block at 618-624 is commented 'FIX-13 skip census + crash-rate ceiling' but only sums skip reasons into results['skips']; it never computes a per-dial crash/skip rate, never compares to the ceiling, and never flags or halts. Worse, the output has no per-dial denominator (total attempted readings), so the crash rate cannot even be reconstructed downstream from results['skips']. The protocol requires 'a max exception rate per dial (5%) → exceed = investigate, never silently proceed.' On 211 gappy/penny/delisted names the narrowed except in _positions (line 206) will fire non-randomly per (dial,name) = uncontrolled censoring, which is exactly what this ceiling was meant to catch. The narrowing itself (catch ValueError/KeyError/ZeroDivisionError/IndexError, else propagate) conforms, but the enforcement gate does not exist.
findings.protocol_ref: §3.1 FIX-13; §11 'allowed skip-reason set + 5% max exception rate'

findings.title: FIX-24 census-balance (and survivor-count) gating is MISSING — shards carry no strata to gate on
findings.detail: Protocol §4/§5 FIX-24 requires a regime/base may only SET a dial if valid assets are '≥ M_min AND census-balanced', and any dial/regime whose JND rests on <M_min non-survivors must be flagged 'survivor-calibrated'. The code's only eligibility test (_jnd_candidates, lines 486-489) is n_valid>=M_MIN, sat_ok, and boots is not None. There is no census-balance computation at all. The shards (verified) store only `alive` per asset — no cap_bucket / vol_bucket / sector — and Phase B never joins panel.json metadata, so census balance across the 6 cap×vol macro-cells is not even computable as written. n_delisted is recorded in each candidate (pts[i_cross][4]) but nothing gates or flags on it. A dial whose crossing rests on a thin single-stratum or survivor-only sliver can therefore set the frozen grid unchecked — either too fine (unrepresentative tail → vacuity) or survivor-coarse (unsafe).
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:460-490 (_jnd_candidates, no census); shard keys = symbol,ticker_id,alive,records,... (no strata)
findings.protocol_ref: §5 FIX-24(a) census-balanced; §5 'survivor-calibrated' flag; §2.4

findings.title: FIX-25 temporal halves are reported but never ADOPTED — code comment claims adoption that does not exist
findings.detail: Protocol FIX-25: 're-derive the aggregate JND on 2000-2015 and 2016-2023 ... If they diverge beyond one grid step, ADOPT the finer and flag regime-sequence-sensitivity.' The code (lines 588-600) computes halves['half_1']/['half_2'] as the min crossing per half and stores them in results['dials'][key]['temporal_halves'], but there is NO comparison of the two halves, NO divergence test, and NO path that folds the finer half into the selected JND (REGIME_KEYS excludes the temporal keys). The inline comment at line 588 literally says 'adopt finer if > 1 step apart' — but no such code follows, making it a misleading (deviates-silent) claim. If a temporal half is genuinely finer, ignoring it leaves the grid coarser than the data supports = the primary unsafe direction.
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:588-608 (halves stored, comment claims adopt); REGIME_KEYS at :77 excludes half_1/half_2
findings.protocol_ref: §5 FIX-25 'adopt the finer'

findings.title: FIX-19 extend-finer re-measure loop not implemented — detection only, EXTEND_FINER constant is dead
findings.detail: Protocol FIX-19 mandates a deterministic pre-committed response: if a dial's JND lands on the finest sweep rung for any base/regime, PREPEND finer rungs (RATIOS+[0.02,0.03], threshold+[0.5], mult+[0.05]) and RE-MEASURE until the JND is strictly interior — 'My step grid bottomed out can never be accepted as fine-enough.' The code only DETECTS (at_floor → needs_finer, lines 585-587) and prints a message (738-740). EXTEND_FINER (line 126) is referenced nowhere; run_phase_a is called without the `extra` arg (line 727) and main never populates extra_ratios from needs_finer, so even a manual re-run would not inject the finer rungs. Since a bottomed-out dial means the true JND is finer than measured (grid too coarse → N under-counted → gate too lenient = unsafe), an operator who proceeds on a results file with a non-empty needs_finer list ships the unsafe direction. The resolution is manual and the wiring to perform it is absent.

findings.title: Section-6 bootstrap convergence criterion and subsample ladder are MISSING while the docstring/manifest claim them
findings.detail: The module docstring (lines 23-24) states Phase B performs 'section-6 stability (bootstrap convergence + subsample ladder)', and the results manifest surfaces SUBSAMPLE_SIZES/SUBSAMPLE_SEED (line 569) as if used. Neither is implemented: (1) _bootstrap_jnd (493-517) produces a boot distribution used ONLY for the boot_ci90 percentile; the §6 convergence test ('≥90% of resamples on the point step or adjacent finer; coarser-by-≥1-step in >10% = instability flag') is computed and reported nowhere. (2) The strata-preserving subsample ladder {50,75,100,125,150} and its stopping rule are not implemented anywhere — SUBSAMPLE_SIZES/SEED are constants surfaced in the manifest but never consumed. So the pre-registered evidence that turns '≥150 names' into a stability curve does not exist, and the output overstates what was done.
findings.protocol_ref: §6 convergence (≥90% within one step) + subsample ladder + stopping rule; §11 Stability

findings.title: FIX-18 monotonicity diagnostic not computed (MONO_TOL dead) — the load-bearing 'finer-is-safe' premise is unverified
findings.detail: The sustained/interpolated crossing (_crossing, 397-412) is correctly implemented and robust to dips. But FIX-18 also requires a pre-registered monotonicity DIAGNOSTIC: 'fraction of assets whose δ(step) is non-monotone beyond tolerance', reported so the finer-is-safe direction is proven under the verified premise (§4 calls this equivalence 'load-bearing'). MONO_TOL=0.02 (line 99) is referenced nowhere and no non-monotonicity fraction is emitted. The mitigation (sustained crossing) is present; the certifying diagnostic is absent, so the audit trail that the aggregation equivalence holds is missing from the frozen output.
findings.protocol_ref: §3.1 FIX-18; §4 monotonicity equivalence

findings.title: Pre-run hard gates absent: FIX-12 full-vs-crisis δ assertion and §3 self-vs-self determinism gate
findings.detail: FIX-12 is correctly implemented — I verified from the smoke shards that regime slices differ from full (e.g., shard 47/ACAD rsi_reversion.period base14 r0.05: full δ=0.081 vs gfc δ=0.003; sma_crossover.fast_period base15 r0.05: full 0.011 vs calm_a 0.015), so the window-keyed cache and slicing are real, not collapsed. However the protocol also mandates two pre-run HARD GATES that would catch a regression: (a) FIX-12 'full-window vs a crisis-window δ for ≥1 high-vol name must differ by a pre-registered minimum — a zero difference fails the run'; (b) §3 'Determinism (self-vs-self=0.0) re-asserted as a hard pre-run gate on the full panel including every delisted/gappy name'. Neither assertion exists in the code. The fixes work today but nothing fails the 450k run loudly if a future edit reintroduces the cache-window bug or a nondeterminism.
findings.protocol_ref: §3.1 FIX-12 pre-run assertion; §3 determinism hard pre-run gate

findings.title: FIX-11 K-seed draw-level JND spread is not measured by this engine (deferred/absent)
findings.detail: FIX-11 requires the recommended JND per dial be reported across K=5 alternate panel seeds so 'no one can shop a single seed that yields a coarser grid'. This measurement engine consumes only manifest['panel'] (the primary panel, line 709) and has no loop to run the committed K-seed alternate panels through the tensor→aggregation to report cross-seed JND spread. Whether this is intentionally deferred (each alt panel is another ~450k-backtest run) or simply omitted, the draw-level anti-shopping guard is not present in the frozen measurement output, and nothing in the results flags its absence.
findings.protocol_ref: §2.4 FIX-11; §11 Seeds (K-seed draw-level stability)

findings.title: --smoke global WINDOWS/REGIME_KEYS reassignment does not reach ProcessPool workers (Windows spawn)
findings.detail: main() sets `global WINDOWS/REGIME_KEYS/TEMPORAL_KEYS` for --smoke (lines 721-724), but Phase-A workers run under ProcessPoolExecutor, which on Windows uses spawn: each worker re-imports the module and reads the module-level 8-window WINDOWS, not the parent's reassignment (scan_asset reads WINDOWS as a global, and only `dials` is passed as an argument). Confirmed empirically: the smoke shards (47/105/143) contain gfc/calm_a/calm_b/half_1/half_2 records despite smoke restricting to {full,covid}; Phase B (parent) then aggregates only the 2 reassigned windows. The real run is unaffected (constants are never mutated), so this is cosmetic/perf for smoke — but it means the smoke path does NOT faithfully exercise the reduced-window configuration, and it is a latent trap if any future runtime global-mutation is relied on in workers.
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:721-724 (global reassignment in parent); 282 & 370-372 (scan_asset reads WINDOWS global under ProcessPool)
findings.protocol_ref: n/a (smoke fidelity / spawn semantics)

findings.title: SKIP_REASONS enumerated set is never validated; FIX-16 warmup-material check omitted
findings.detail: Two smaller deviations-disclosed: (1) SKIP_REASONS (line 130) is defined but never checked against emitted statuses; the actual FIX-13 guard is the narrowed except (line 206), so a status typo or a new skip reason would not be caught by the pre-registered set. (2) FIX-16 offers min-bars OR a warmup-material check with warmup-bar discard; the code implements only the dial-specific min-bars gate `len(data) >= WARMUP_K*max(base,stepped)` (lines 302-311, correctly using max) and never trims the warmup region from the position vectors, so the first ~period ffilled/warming bars still enter the disagreement comparison. Both are defensible IMPL choices but neither the min-bars-only decision nor the un-run warmup-material check is surfaced as a disclosed deviation in the manifest.
findings.protocol_ref: §3.1 FIX-13 skip-reason set; §3.1 FIX-16 warmup check

result.summary:
Protocol-conformance review of the V3 coverage-calibration engine before its ~450k-backtest run. The expensive Phase-A path (window-keyed cache FIX-12, degenerate/saturation classification FIX-14, N_min=100 both-sides FIX-15, warmup max(base,stepped) FIX-16, delisted-tail trim gated on in-window death FIX-17, narrowed instrumented except FIX-13, full-window return-correlation streams FIX-27, multi_indicator raw-exposure §9) is largely CONFORMING — I verified from the smoke shards that regime slices genuinely differ from full-window results, so FIX-12 is real. The defects are concentrated in Phase B (cheap, re-derivable — the backtests are NOT wasted) and in unenforced pre-registered guards. ONE BLOCKER: _ci_separated_min is a no-op — it provably returns the raw min over all ~18 base×regime cells in every case (verified live), so FIX-20's winner's-curse correction is never applied and the write-once frozen N is biased artifactually fine, contradicting both the function's own docstring and §4. Four HIGH deviations: MAX_CRASH_RATE defined but never enforced or computable (FIX-13); census-balance + survivor-count gating entirely missing and uncomputable because shards carry no strata (FIX-24); temporal halves reported but never adopted despite an inline comment claiming adoption (FIX-25); extend-finer re-measure loop unimplemented with EXTEND_FINER dead (FIX-19); and section-6 bootstrap-convergence + subsample-ladder claimed by the docstring/manifest but absent. Plus medium/low items: monotonicity diagnostic not computed (MONO_TOL dead), no pre-run determinism/full-vs-crisis hard gates, K-seed spread not measured, and a Windows-spawn smoke-fidelity trap. Net safety direction: several defects (blocker, FIX-19, FIX-25) push toward an under-strict grid or lock in un-stripped artifactual fineness — the exact failure modes the pre-registration exists to prevent. Fix Phase B and wire the missing guards before writing/hashing calibration-v3-results.json.</summary>
</invoke>

findings.title: _ci_separated_min never resolves to the runner-up — it always returns the raw finest (FIX-20 winner's-curse guard is inert)
findings.protocol_ref: §4 FIX-20 / §11 'CI-separated-min over bases and regimes'; known-IMPL 'resolves unseparated ties to the runner-up point estimate'
findings.detail: The function is THE frozen cross-base/cross-regime estimator that turns ~18 noisy tail-quantiles into one JND. It is supposed to only let a finer candidate win if its bootstrap upper-CI beats the runner-up's point estimate, and otherwise fall back to the RUNNER-UP (coarser). It does neither. Trace: after sorting eligible ascending, the loop's first iteration tests the finest correctly, but on failure sets winner=None and continues; every later candidate's `rest` still INCLUDES the finer candidate(s), so `cand.boot_ci90 <= min(rest.jnd)` compares against an even smaller jnd and is essentially never true — winner stays None to the end, then the `if winner is None` branch sets `winner = eligible[0]` (the FINEST, not the runner-up). I verified numerically: for finest-unseparated inputs the returned jnd == raw_min in every case (separated=False), identical to the separated case. Consequence: `selected.jnd == raw_min` unconditionally; the entire CI-separation / winner's-curse correction the protocol pre-registers (bias ~0.85σ for k=3, ~1.8σ for k=18, multiplicative across dials into N) is silently absent, and the frozen N-input is biased fine with no bound. The docstring itself says the unseparated case should resolve 'to the runner-up' — the code contradicts its own docstring and the stated IMPL. Fix in Phase B only (no 450k re-run needed) but MUST be fixed before the write-once freeze, or the frozen number is wrong and unretunable.

findings.title: _bootstrap_jnd resamples every step INDEPENDENTLY — breaks within-asset curve dependence and understates the CI the separation gate rides on
findings.protocol_ref: §6 'resample the asset axis of the committed δ-tensor with replacement, re-run the entire §4–§5 aggregation per resample'
findings.detail: The protocol's bootstrap resamples the ASSET AXIS once per replicate and re-derives the whole curve, preserving that a high-vol asset is a big-mover at every step. The code instead draws a fresh `rng.integers(0,len(deltas),len(deltas))` INDEPENDENTLY inside the per-step loop, so the positive cross-step correlation of a per-asset curve is destroyed and the whole-curve shifts that dominate crossing-location variance are lost. I demonstrated the crossing-SD is understated (~10-13% in synthetic panels, growing with tail heterogeneity — exactly the high-vol tail that governs Q=0.85). The correct joint resample is feasible with the data on hand: _collect already stores `wc['assets']` (asset IDs) per step (line 424-426) but _bootstrap_jnd ignores them and uses only `wc['deltas']`; the per-step delta arrays are also different asset subsets, so a naive shared-index cannot be applied — you must resample asset IDs from the union and index a per-asset-by-step structure. Direction: understated boot_ci90 → the FIX-20 separation test (once un-inerted per the blocker) becomes too lenient and admits noise-driven fineness, the very thing FIX-20 exists to reject. Also corrupts the reported CIs and the `ci_separated` flag today.

findings.title: _crossing idx==0 returns steps[0] with no 'unresolved' signal, and the extend-finer guard compares to the GLOBAL finest ratio, not the per-base realized floor
findings.protocol_ref: §3.1 FIX-18 (sustained crossing) + FIX-19 ('my step grid bottomed out can never be accepted as fine-enough')
findings.detail: When D_Q is already ≥T at the smallest SAMPLED step, `_crossing` returns steps[0] — indistinguishable to the caller from a genuine interpolated crossing that happens to land on steps[0]. The true JND is ≤steps[0] (unresolved), i.e. reported coarser than reality. FIX-19 is supposed to catch this, but `at_floor = sel.jnd <= finest` uses `finest = min(RATIOS) = 0.05` (a global constant), while for small period bases 0.05 rounds onto the base and is dropped, so the per-base realized floor is coarser: I confirmed sma_crossover.fast_period base=7 → smallest realized rung is r0.08 (value 8), macd_cross.fast base=6 → r0.12, base=10 → r0.08 (and shard 47 shows exactly these truncated label sets). So an idx==0 candidate at 0.08/0.12 is reported as a resolved JND and, if it is the finest ELIGIBLE candidate in its regime (eligibility is per base×regime and depends on saturation/valid-n, so the r0.05-realizing base can be ineligible while the small base is eligible), the dial's selected jnd = 0.08/0.12 and at_floor (≤0.05) does NOT fire → a bottomed-out, coarse-in-the-unsafe-direction JND is frozen without triggering the extend-finer re-run. Fix: have _crossing flag idx==0 as unresolved (force needs_finer for that dial), and scope the floor test per-curve, not to the global 0.05.

findings.title: Monotonicity diagnostic (FIX-18) is not computed — MONO_TOL is defined but never used, so the §4 equivalence proof that the aggregation rests on is unverified
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:99 (MONO_TOL defined; grep shows no other use)
findings.protocol_ref: §3.1 FIX-18 ('pre-register a monotonicity diagnostic (fraction of assets whose δ(step) is non-monotone beyond tolerance) and report it'); §4 'this equivalence is why the monotonicity diagnostic is load-bearing'
findings.detail: §4 proves that taking the per-step Q-quantile then first-crossing T equals reading the (1−Q) percentile of the per-ASSET JND distribution ONLY under per-asset monotonicity of δ(step); the doc calls this 'load-bearing' and says 'the finer-is-safe direction is proven only under the verified monotonicity premise.' The code computes no such diagnostic — MONO_TOL=0.02 is surfaced in the results manifest constants (line 99 → implied applied) but nothing measures per-asset δ(step) monotonicity or reports the non-monotone fraction. Without it, (a) the interpretation of D_Q crossings as JND percentiles is asserted, not checked, and (b) the manifest advertises a guard that never ran (a provenance/honesty gap that a skeptic re-deriving N cannot audit). The sustained-crossing logic makes the D_Q CURVE robust to dips, but that is a different object than per-asset monotonicity and does not substitute for the diagnostic.

findings.title: FIX-25 temporal 'adopt the finer' response is not wired — temporal halves are reported but never compared, diverge-checked, or adopted
findings.protocol_ref: §5 FIX-25 ('if they diverge beyond one grid step, adopt the finer and flag regime-sequence-sensitivity')
findings.detail: The code computes per-dial half_1 / half_2 crossings (raw min over bases, no CI separation) and stores them in temporal_halves, matching the code comment 'report; adopt finer if > 1 step apart'. But there is no comparison of half_1 vs half_2, no >1-grid-step divergence test, and no adoption of the finer half into the frozen JND. FIX-25's adopt-finer is a pre-registered SAFETY response (finer = safe); its absence means a dial whose 2000–2015 vs 2016–2023 JND diverge is left at the possibly-coarser full-panel value with only a passive report, i.e. the frozen N can stay coarser (unsafe) than the temporal check would demand, with no flag.

findings.title: §6 stability machinery (subsample ladder + bootstrap convergence) is not implemented, though its constants are advertised in the manifest
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:97-98 (SUBSAMPLE_SIZES/SEED defined; grep shows only manifest echo at 569)
findings.protocol_ref: §6 (bootstrap convergence ≥90% within one step; strata-preserving subsample ladder {50,75,100,125,150,full} + stopping rule); §11 'Stability' freeze item
findings.detail: Phase B computes bootstrap CIs inside _jnd_candidates but never evaluates the §6 convergence criterion (bootstrapped JND on the point step or adjacent-finer in ≥90% of resamples; coarser-by-≥1-step in >10% = instability flag) and never runs the subsample ladder (SUBSAMPLE_SIZES / SUBSAMPLE_SEED=20230707 are only echoed into results['constants']). The pre-registered 'turn ≥150 into evidence' outputs (JND-vs-effective-n curve, stopping rule, instability flags, effective-tail CI) are therefore not produced, while the manifest lists the ladder seed/sizes as if applied. As with the monotonicity diagnostic, this is both a missing statistical check and a provenance mismatch (advertised-but-not-run).

findings.title: Integer-governed analytic rule uses any(base*sel<1) — collapses per-base/mixed structure and mislabels ratio-governed sma/macd as integer-governed
findings.protocol_ref: §7 FIX-31 / §9 ('classify integer-governed iff base×r<1 integer … structural, robust to quantile'; pre-committed response to sma/macd flips is KEEP the ratio grid, not one-cell-per-integer)
findings.detail: `analytic = any(b*sel<1 for b in bases)` marks a dial integer-governed if the JND ratio times ANY base is <1. Because bases include small values (5–7) and JND ratios are ~0.05–0.5, base×sel<1 is almost always true at the smallest base (e.g. sma_crossover.fast base=7: 7×~0.1<1), so ratio-governed dials that the shipped v2 check (validate_grid_noise.py: 'sma/macd periods ratio-governed', 'bollinger mixed') established as ratio/mixed get a single integer-governed=True. This is the exact one-cell-per-integer N-explosion FIX-31 is designed to prevent, arriving via the analytic rule rather than the empirical one. The structural rule should be evaluated per base (report which bases are integer- vs ratio-governed, i.e. surface 'mixed') or against the governing base that actually set sel — not collapsed with `any`. It is reported-only here, but it is the analytic PRIMARY that FIX-31 says drives the by-hand coverage.py transcription.

findings.title: Single-eligible candidate is flagged ci_separated=True, and non-crossing bootstrap resamples are censored to an un-pre-registered steps[-1]*2
findings.protocol_ref: §4 FIX-20; §6 (bootstrap)
findings.detail: Two smaller correctness/honesty items. (1) When only one candidate is eligible (all other bases/regimes ineligible or non-crossing), the loop's `not rest` branch declares winner with separated=True — reporting statistical separation where there is no comparator; a dial whose JND rests on a single base×regime is then labelled robustly-separated. (2) _bootstrap_jnd censors non-crossing resamples to `steps[-1]*2` — an arbitrary 2× multiplier that the protocol never specifies; it determines how much a non-crossing inflates boot_ci90 and thus (once the blocker is fixed) the separation decision, yet it is not in the pre-registered freeze list. Both are minor while the CI is inert per the blocker, but should be corrected/pre-registered alongside the FIX-20 repair.

result.summary: Adversarial statistics-correctness review of the V3 coverage-calibration engine (Phase-B aggregation math) before the ~450k-backtest run. One BLOCKER: _ci_separated_min (the frozen cross-base/regime estimator) always returns the raw finest candidate and never resolves unseparated ties to the runner-up — I verified numerically that selected.jnd == raw_min in every branch, so the pre-registered FIX-20 winner's-curse correction is completely inert and the frozen N-input is biased fine (~0.85σ–1.8σ, multiplicative into N) with no bound. Three HIGHs: (a) _bootstrap_jnd resamples each step independently, breaking within-asset curve dependence and understating the CI (~10-13% in synthetic panels, worse on the fat tail that governs Q=0.85) — the fix is feasible since asset IDs are stored but unused; (b) _crossing's idx==0 branch reports steps[0] as a resolved JND with no unresolved signal, and the extend-finer guard tests against the global min ratio (0.05) rather than the coarser per-base realized floor (0.08–0.12 for small period bases, confirmed in shard 47), so a bottomed-out coarse JND can freeze without triggering the FIX-19 re-run; (c) the FIX-18 monotonicity diagnostic that the §4 equivalence proof is 'load-bearing' on is never computed (MONO_TOL defined but unused) yet advertised in the manifest. Plus mediums: FIX-25 temporal adopt-finer not wired (report-only), §6 subsample-ladder/bootstrap-convergence unimplemented while advertised, and the integer-rule any(base*sel<1) mislabels ratio-governed sma/macd as integer-governed (N-explosion risk). All are Phase-B-only fixes needing no 450k re-run, but every one must land before the write-once freeze or the frozen, unretunable N-input ships wrong. The smoke shards exercise none of this (3 assets < M_MIN=30 → all dials null), so these paths are untested by the smoke run.

findings.title: _positions catches Python builtins, not the engine's BacktestError hierarchy — every legitimate engine rejection aborts the whole 450k run (and re-aborts on resume)
findings.detail: _positions wraps run_backtest in `except (ValueError, KeyError, ZeroDivisionError, IndexError)` (calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:206). But the engine's typed rejections all inherit from BacktestError(Exception) (engine/exceptions.py:6) — InsufficientDataError, InvalidParameterError, NoTradesError — AND run_backtest wraps any internal backtesting.py failure as `raise BacktestError(...)` (runner.py:220, :292). NONE of these are subclasses of ValueError/KeyError/etc. I verified: issubclass(BacktestError,(ValueError,KeyError,ZeroDivisionError,IndexError))==False, and drove a BacktestError through _positions live — it PROPAGATED uncaught ('PROPAGATED (uncaught by _positions): BacktestError ...'). Consequence: the intended FIX-13 'expected engine rejections return status=crashed' path is DEAD for the actual engine errors. When any of the 450k backtests hits a real engine rejection (highly likely on penny/delisted/gappy names with warmup_bars=0 and extreme param combos — line 292 wraps every backtesting.py internal failure), it propagates _positions -> scan_asset -> run_phase_a's `except Exception` which prints FAILED and RE-RAISES (:378-381), killing the entire ProcessPoolExecutor. Because the shard is written only AFTER scan_asset returns, the failing asset's shard is never written; the run is resume-safe by skipping existing shards, so on every restart the same asset is retried and re-raises identically — Phase A can never complete, and you cannot drop the name because the panel sha256 is frozen (:711-714). This is the opposite of FIX-13's intent (which names BacktestError-family as the EXPECTED errors to classify as crashed). Fix: catch BacktestError (import from engine.exceptions) in the _positions except tuple so engine rejections classify as status='crashed' instead of aborting.
findings.protocol_ref: FIX-13 (§3.1): 'narrow the except to expected engine errors ... expected engine rejections return status=crashed with the type'

findings.title: _ci_separated_min never resolves unseparated ties to the runner-up — it always returns the finest eligible (== raw_min), so the FIX-20 CI-separation guard is inert
findings.detail: The stated IMPL choice and the frozen protocol both require: when the finest candidate's bootstrap upper-CI is NOT below the runner-up's point estimate, resolve to the RUNNER-UP (coarser) point estimate. The code does the opposite. Trace of _ci_separated_min (calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:520-548): eligible is sorted ascending by jnd. Iteration 1 tests the finest (eligible[0]); if separated it breaks with winner=eligible[0]. If NOT separated it sets winner=None and continues. For every subsequent candidate cand=eligible[k>=1], `rest` includes eligible[0] whose jnd is the global minimum, so `min(c['jnd'] for c in rest)` == eligible[0].jnd, and the test `cand['boot_ci90'] <= eligible[0].jnd` is essentially never true (an upper CI of a larger point estimate cannot fall below the smallest point estimate). So the loop exits with winner=None, and the fallback at :539-540 sets winner=eligible[0] — the FINEST — not the runner-up. Result: selected['jnd'] == eligible[0]['jnd'] == raw_min in BOTH branches; only the ci_separated boolean differs. The documented/pre-registered 'resolve to runner-up' branch is unreachable dead logic. Impact: FIX-20's winner's-curse correction (min over ~18 noisy base×regime tail-quantiles, biased fine by ~0.85–1.8σ) is completely un-applied — exactly the SECONDARY danger §0 calls out ('the minimum of several noisy estimates ... overcounts N ... vacuous gate'). The committed 'raw-min vs CI-separated-min' audit (required by FIX-20/§11 to make the selection bias visible) emits two identical numbers, falsely implying the separation guard fired. The grid ships finer than the protocol specifies, un-budgeted and multiplicative into N. Fix: on non-separation set winner=eligible[1] (runner-up point estimate) with separated=False, per the docstring and the stated IMPL choice.
findings.protocol_ref: §4 FIX-20 / §5 FIX-24 / §11 Aggregation ('CI-separated-min over bases and regimes'); §0 secondary danger; stated IMPL: 'CI-separated-min resolves unseparated ties to the runner-up point estimate'

findings.title: A single WarehouseDataError (or any non-enumerated exception) mid-run aborts the whole ProcessPool and permanently blocks resume — no quarantine, and the frozen panel hash makes the name un-removable
findings.detail: scan_asset calls warehouse_fetch at its top, OUTSIDE any try (calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:274). warehouse_fetch raises WarehouseDataError (RuntimeError) on the FIX-1 spike guard, nonpositive close, nonfinite ratio, pre-2000 leak, or empty rows (_warehouse.py:61-108). Such a raise propagates scan_asset -> run_phase_a `except Exception` -> re-raise (:378-381), killing every in-flight parallel future and aborting the run. Same permanent-resume trap as the _positions blocker: the shard is never written, the name is retried on every restart, and it cannot be excluded because manifest['panel_sha256'] is asserted at startup (:711-714) so editing the panel breaks the freeze. The panel was pre-gated with the same AM-7 spike fingerprint so this is low-probability, but AM-7 itself documents that adjc/c rounding noise makes naive validity tests false-fail ~50% of names — if the in-run guard and the draw-time guard ever diverge by a bar, one name kills the whole expensive run with no recovery path short of a code edit. Recommend: wrap the per-name scan in a quarantine that writes a flagged/empty shard (recorded skip) rather than aborting, so one bad name is censored-and-logged (FIX-13's own 'record the skip reason' philosophy) instead of halting the batch.
findings.protocol_ref: FIX-13 (record skip, don't silently proceed) vs. resume-safety claim in the module docstring; AM-7

findings.title: FIX-25 temporal-halves are computed and reported but never compared to the selected JND nor used to 'adopt the finer' — the pre-registered adoption branch is absent
findings.detail: run_phase_b computes halves[half_1]/halves[half_2] as raw min-over-bases crossings and stores them under dials[key]['temporal_halves'] (calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:588-600,608). The protocol FIX-25 requires: 'If they diverge beyond one grid step, adopt the finer and flag regime-sequence-sensitivity.' There is no code that (a) compares the halves to the selected JND, (b) tests the >1-grid-step divergence, or (c) adopts the finer value into results['dials'][key]['selected']. The inline comment at :588 even says 'adopt finer if > 1 step apart' but no such logic exists. Net effect: a temporal split that demands a finer grid is reported-only and silently NOT adopted — a coarse-direction miss (the unsafe direction §0 exists to prevent). Either wire the adopt-finer comparison or explicitly downgrade FIX-25 to report-only in the protocol before freezing.
findings.protocol_ref: §5 FIX-25 ('re-derive on 2000–2015 and 2016–2023 ... if they diverge beyond one grid step, adopt the finer')

findings.title: FIX-19 extend-finer is unwired: EXTEND_FINER is dead, main() never passes `extra`, no CLI to inject finer rungs, and write-once blocks a second results write
findings.detail: EXTEND_FINER (calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:126) is defined but referenced nowhere else. The `extra` -> scan_asset extra_ratios plumbing exists (:299,358,372) but main() calls run_phase_a WITHOUT extra (:727) and adds no CLI flag to supply finer ratios. So when Phase B appends a dial to results['needs_finer'] (:585-587) — the pre-committed FIX-19 'grid bottomed out, must re-run finer' signal — there is no executable path to actually prepend [0.02,0.03]/[0.5]/[0.05] and re-measure. Compounding it, FIX-32 write-once refuses to overwrite calibration-v3-results.json (:731-733), so even a manual re-run cannot re-emit results without --force-style intervention. If any dial's JND lands on the finest rung after the 450k run, the operator is stuck without a code edit. Wire EXTEND_FINER into a re-measure path (and a matching CLI arg + shard-force for the affected dials) before the run, or the FIX-19 guarantee ('my step grid bottomed out can never be accepted') is unenforceable.
findings.protocol_ref: §3.1 FIX-19 (extend-finer deterministic response)

findings.title: Exposure fraction divides masked active-bar count by the UNMASKED window length for trimmed delisted names → expo understated → over-flags degenerate
findings.detail: In _delta, act_a/act_b count active bars UNDER the trim mask (calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:241-242), but expo_a/expo_b divide by n_window = len(data), the FULL unmasked window length (:247-248, passed as len(data) at :322). For delisted-tail names where _trim_mask drops up to TRIM_K+stale-run bars, the numerator excludes trimmed active bars while the denominator still includes them, so exposure is biased low. Since the degenerate flag fires on min(expo_a,expo_b) < DEGEN_EXPOSURE (0.02) (:257-259), trimmed names are slightly more likely to be dropped as degenerate, thinning D_Q samples for exactly the survivorship-tail names Q=0.85 leans on. Effect is small (only delisted-tail names, only a handful of trimmed bars) but the numerator/denominator scopes are inconsistent; divide by the masked length m.sum() for consistency.
findings.protocol_ref: FIX-14/FIX-17 (degenerate exposure floor over trimmed vectors)

result.summary: Engine-wiring review of the V3 calibration harness before its ~450k-backtest run found two blockers. (1) _positions catches only Python builtins (ValueError/KeyError/ZeroDivisionError/IndexError) but every engine rejection inherits from BacktestError(Exception) — I drove a BacktestError through _positions live and it propagated uncaught; in the real run it re-raises out of run_phase_a and kills the whole ProcessPool, and because the failing shard is never written the resume-safe restart re-hits the same name forever while the frozen panel hash forbids removing it. This is the exact opposite of FIX-13's intent (BacktestError-family ARE the expected 'crashed' errors). (2) _ci_separated_min never resolves unseparated ties to the runner-up: sorted ascending, the non-separation fallback sets winner=eligible[0] (the finest), so selected['jnd'] always equals raw_min and the FIX-20 winner's-curse guard is inert — the committed 'raw-min vs CI-separated-min' audit emits two identical numbers, and the grid ships finer than the pre-registered protocol (the §0 secondary vacuity danger). The frozen metric wiring itself (cache key with 4dp rounding + window, entry-inclusive/exit-exclusive trade reconstruction, warmup_bars=0/exclusive_orders/trade_on_close/cash/commission, union-in-market delta, _trim_mask run-end indexing, tail_here logic, _strategy_returns alignment for the symmetric correlation matrix, and str(dict(ptup)) deterministic label ordering) all match v2 or are correct. One high (any WarehouseDataError mid-run aborts the batch with the same permanent-resume trap; recommend quarantine) plus two mediums (FIX-25 temporal-halves and FIX-19 extend-finer are both computed/flagged but not wired to adopt-finer) and one low (expo normalization mixes masked numerator with unmasked denominator for trimmed names).

findings.title: Shard resume keyed only on file existence silently reuses 2-dial SMOKE shards in a full run
findings.detail: run_phase_a resumes with `if force or not f.exists()` (calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:363-364) and SMOKE writes real `{ticker_id}.json` shards into the SAME SHARD_DIR (`--smoke` only redirects the *results* file to smoke-results.json; run_phase_a always writes to SHARD_DIR). The three smoke shards for the first 3 panel names ALREADY EXIST on disk right now: 47.json (ACAD), 105.json (ADNT), 143.json (AEYE). I verified each contains records for only 2 of the 10 tradeable dials (sma_crossover.fast_period, rsi_reversion.period) because smoke restricts `dials` (line 719). A full run would therefore SKIP all three assets (I simulated the resume decision: skips [47,105,143]), and phase B (_collect) reads them as complete shards. For the 8 dials absent from those shards, assets ACAD/ADNT/AEYE contribute ZERO records with no error — the pre-registered 211-name study silently becomes 208 for 8 of 10 dials, and the SAME 3 specific names are always the missing ones (deterministic bias, not random). This corrupts a frozen measurement and breaks the FIX-32 'byte-identical re-run' guarantee (result now depends on whether a smoke run happened first). Concrete mitigations: (1) delete the 3 existing smoke shards before the real run; (2) make --smoke write to a dedicated dir (e.g. SHARD_DIR/smoke/) or a smoke-prefixed filename so smoke and full never share files; (3) have resume validate that an existing shard covers the full expected dial×window set before skipping (see provenance finding).
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:363-364,730 (SHARD_DIR write) + smoke dials line 719; existing shards data/calibration_v3_shards/{47,105,143}.json
findings.protocol_ref: FIX-32 write-once/byte-identical; §8 'commit the δ-tensor once'

findings.title: --smoke mutates module globals (WINDOWS/REGIME_KEYS/TEMPORAL_KEYS) that spawned Phase-A workers never see; smoke computes 7-8 windows, not 2
findings.detail: main() mutates the module globals WINDOWS/REGIME_KEYS/TEMPORAL_KEYS in-process (lines 721-724) to shrink smoke to full+covid. On Windows the ProcessPoolExecutor uses spawn: workers re-import the module and get the ORIGINAL 8-window WINDOWS dict — the mutation is confined to the parent. Evidence: smoke shard 47.json contains 7 windows (full,gfc,covid,calm_a,calm_b,half_1,half_2 — dotcom absent only because ACAD has no 2000-02 bars), NOT the 2 the smoke path intends; meanwhile smoke-results.json (phase B, which DOES run in the parent and sees the mutation) reports only [covid,full]. So within a single --smoke invocation phase A and phase B disagree on the window set. Consequences: (a) --smoke is not the cheap wiring check it claims (it runs ~4x the intended backtests over 7-8 windows), so it gives false confidence that the reduced-window code path works — that path is effectively never exercised; (b) it is the mechanism that makes the smoke shards look full-window-complete on their 2 dials, amplifying the resume blocker above. The full run itself is unaffected (it never mutates globals), but any future reliance on cross-process global mutation is broken by design. Fix: pass windows/regime/temporal selections as explicit arguments to scan_asset (like `dials` already is), never via global mutation.
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:721-724 (global mutation), :282 `for wkey in WINDOWS`, :372 ex.submit passes dials as arg but not windows
findings.protocol_ref: §5 frozen windows / regime list

findings.title: Shards carry no constants/code/grid-version provenance; a changed constant between partial runs silently mixes old+new shards
findings.detail: Each shard dict (scan_asset return, :277) is {symbol,ticker_id,alive,records,correlations,multi_exposure,trim} — no grid_version, no constants hash, no code fingerprint. Many pre-registered constants are baked into Phase A and frozen into the shard records: DEGEN_EXPOSURE/DEGEN_ROUNDTRIPS (the `degenerate` flag), N_MIN_ACTIVE (active_lt_nmin), WARMUP_K (bars_lt_warmup_k), TRIM_K/STALE_RUN (trim mask), SAT_CEILING is applied in phase B but the delta/degenerate classification is in phase A. If any of these changes mid-workstream (crash + edit + resume, or a re-run after tweaking a value), run_phase_a skips already-written shards (they exist) so old-constant shards persist and are aggregated together with new-constant shards — undetectably. The results file records only the CURRENT process's `constants` block (:562-573), which will not match the constants that actually produced the reused shards. This defeats FIX-32: 'a re-run is byte-identical or fails the freeze-hash' is only true if nothing changed AND no partial shards predate the change; there is no record of WHICH shard set produced the results. Fix: stamp each shard with a hash of the frozen Phase-A constants (+ optionally git SHA), and have resume/phase-B refuse or force-recompute shards whose stamp differs; record the shard-set hash in the results manifest.
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:277 (shard has no version), :562-573 (results.constants from current process), :364 (resume ignores content)
findings.protocol_ref: FIX-32 write-once + §11 top-level freeze hash over the results artifact

findings.title: warehouse_fetch is called outside the try/except in scan_asset; one adjustment/data failure aborts the whole multi-hour run with no 'adjustment-excluded' skip record
findings.detail: scan_asset calls warehouse_fetch at :274 BEFORE any try block. warehouse_fetch (._warehouse.py) can raise WarehouseDataError from _assert_ratio_valid (FIX-1 nonpositive close / nonfinite ratio / spike), assert_post2000 (FIX-2), resolve_ticker, or 'no bars'. Any such raise propagates: scan_asset raises -> fut.result() raises in run_phase_a -> the `except Exception: ... raise` (:378-381) re-raises and kills the entire run after however many hours. The protocol pre-registers 'adjustment-excluded' as an ALLOWED skip reason (FIX-13 skip set) — i.e. a name failing adjustment validity should be recorded and skipped, not abort the run — but that behavior is not implemented for the whole-asset fetch path (only per-(dial,step) engine crashes are caught in _positions:206). Probability is LOW on this panel: I verified all 211 names have ratio_spikes=0 and passed AM-2, so the runtime FIX-1 spike guard is unlikely to trip; but the consequence (hours lost, the offending asset re-hits the same error every resume and can never complete without a code change) is severe and the protocol-mandated skip is missing. Fix: (1) pre-flight all 211 warehouse_fetch calls (no backtests, cheap) before launching the pool so any data failure surfaces in seconds, not hours; and (2) wrap the fetch in scan_asset and on WarehouseDataError emit a shard/record with skip_reason='adjustment-excluded' instead of propagating.
findings.code_ref: scripts/calibrate_coverage_grid_v3.py:274 (fetch outside try) + :378-381 (fatal re-raise); scripts/_warehouse.py:61-75,104-108 (raise sites)
findings.protocol_ref: FIX-13 allowed skip-reason set {overlap<50, active<N_min, adjustment-excluded, delisted-truncated}

findings.title: A single non-enumerated engine exception on any 1 of 211 assets is fatal and blocks resume until code-fixed
findings.detail: _positions catches only (ValueError, KeyError, ZeroDivisionError, IndexError) -> status='crashed' (:206). Any other exception from run_backtest (TypeError, AttributeError, RuntimeError, AssertionError, pandas/scipy errors) over ~450k backtests on 78 delisted + gappy/penny names propagates out of scan_asset and, via run_phase_a's re-raise (:381), aborts the run. This is FIX-13-intended ('fail loudly on any non-enumerated class'), and completed shards are preserved (written per-future in as_completed, so resume re-runs only unfinished assets) — that is the saving grace. But operationally it means one unlucky non-enumerated exception on a gappy name creates an unrecoverable hard-stop: every resume re-hits the same asset and the same exception, so the 211-name study can never finish without a code change. Given 78 non-survivor names, the chance that at least one trips a non-enumerated path across 450k calls is non-trivial. Recommendation: pre-flight a 1-window smoke over ALL 211 (not 3) before the full run, and/or widen the caught set with a recorded 'crashed:<type>' skip while still respecting the 5% per-dial crash ceiling, so a stray exception censors one (dial,asset) cell rather than killing the run.
findings.protocol_ref: FIX-13 max 5% exception rate per dial; three-outcome classification

findings.title: TQDM_DISABLE is set only in workers, not the main process — benign here but worth noting
findings.detail: _init_worker sets TQDM_DISABLE='1' for each worker (:143). The main process never sets it. This is currently harmless because the main process never invokes the engine: Phase A runs all backtests inside the pool, and Phase B is pure array aggregation with no run_backtest call. So no tqdm bar can originate in main. Flagging only so that if a future refactor ever runs a backtest in the parent (e.g., a --workers 0/serial path or an in-main pre-flight), TQDM_DISABLE must be set at module top-level, not just in _init_worker, to keep the 450k-bar spam out of the console/logs. Also note: Phase B determinism is otherwise sound — _bootstrap_jnd, the family-correlation build, and the skip census all sort tensor.items() or are addition-only, and _dial_curves/_ci_separated_min sort their outputs; the single shared BOOT_SEED rng is threaded through dials in fixed PARAMS insertion order, so results are reproducible provided nobody reorders the dial loop or parallelizes Phase B.
findings.protocol_ref: n/a (ops hygiene)

result.summary: Adversarial ops/resume review of the V3 calibration engine before its ~450k-backtest run. ONE BLOCKER: smoke and full runs share SHARD_DIR and resume skips on bare file existence, so the three smoke shards already on disk (47/105/143, each with only 2 of 10 dials) would be silently reused by a full run — I simulated the skip and confirmed 8 dials would drop those 3 named assets with no error, corrupting the pre-registered study and breaking FIX-32 byte-identical reproducibility. Delete those 3 shards and give smoke its own dir before any real run. THREE HIGHS: (1) --smoke mutates module globals that spawned Phase-A workers never see, so smoke actually computes 7-8 windows not 2 (proven by shard 47 vs smoke-results.json divergence) — it does not exercise the reduced-window path and amplifies the blocker; (2) shards carry no constants/code/version provenance, so a changed constant between partial runs silently mixes old+new shards while the results file records only the current process's constants; (3) warehouse_fetch is outside scan_asset's try, so one adjustment/data failure aborts the whole multi-hour run with no pre-registered 'adjustment-excluded' skip (low probability — all 211 names have ratio_spikes=0 — but catastrophic; pre-flight all 211 fetches first). Plus one medium (non-enumerated engine exception is an unrecoverable hard-stop until code-fixed; completed shards do persist) and one low (TQDM_DISABLE not set in main, benign). Phase B determinism otherwise checks out (sorted/additive aggregation, fixed-order shared rng). All measurement-corruption findings err by shrinking/biasing the panel, which for this safety-asymmetric gate is not automatically the safe (finer) direction — it must be fixed, not waved through.

findings.title: _ci_separated_min always returns the raw finest — FIX-20 winner's-curse correction is completely inert
findings.detail: In the unseparated branch (lines 539-540) the fallback sets winner = eligible[0] (the finest point estimate), and in the separated branch the winner is also eligible[0]. I verified by isolation test: for cands finest=0.08(ci90=0.30) vs runner-up=0.20(ci90=0.35) the function returns jnd=0.08; for the separated variant it also returns jnd=0.08. Therefore sel['jnd'] == sel['raw_min'] in EVERY case, for any dial, base and regime. Consequences: (1) the whole FIX-20/FIX-24 'bootstrap-separated min' — the mechanism that is supposed to strip the ~0.85σ (k=3 bases) to ~1.8σ (k~18 base×regime) winner's-curse fine-bias before it multiplies into N — does nothing; the selected JND is the raw min over ~18 noisy tail-quantiles, exactly the artifact-driven fineness §0 forbids (over-fine → sr0≈√(2·ln N) → vacuous gate → tempts post-hoc coarsening = the back-door unsafe direction). (2) It contradicts the pre-registered IMPL choice ('CI-separated-min resolves unseparated ties to the runner-up point estimate') and the function's own docstring ('resolved to the runner-up ... COARSE-ward between statistically-indistinguishable candidates'): the code should pick eligible[1] (runner-up, coarser) when the finest does not separate, but picks eligible[0]. (3) The 'report raw-min vs CI-separated-min so the selection bias is visible' transparency (FIX-20) is defeated — the two are always equal, misleadingly implying no bias / already-separated even when the correction never ran. This is a wrong measurement that runs on all 10 tradeable dials and directly mis-sizes N; it must be fixed before the 450k-backtest write-once run because the results file is frozen/hashed afterward.
findings.protocol_ref: PROTOCOL §4 FIX-20 / §5 FIX-24 / §11; prompt IMPL note 'unseparated ties → runner-up'

findings.title: MAX_CRASH_RATE (FIX-13 5% per-dial exception ceiling) defined but never enforced or reported as a rate
findings.detail: MAX_CRASH_RATE = 0.05 is declared at line 132 and referenced nowhere else in the file (grep confirms a single occurrence). The skip census (lines 619-624) sums wc['skips'] event counts per dial but never (a) checks any dial against the 5% ceiling, nor (b) emits a per-dial denominator (total attempted (base,step,asset,window) cells), so the crash/skip RATE cannot even be reconstructed from the frozen results file. FIX-13 pre-registers this ceiling precisely because on 211 gappy/penny/delisted names engine rejections fire non-randomly per (dial,name) = uncontrolled censoring; a dial with, say, 30% crashes would silently proceed and set a biased JND with no flag. Wire the ceiling check (fail/flag when crashed-fraction > MAX_CRASH_RATE per dial) and emit per-dial attempt totals before the run.
findings.protocol_ref: PROTOCOL §3.1 FIX-13; §11 pre-registration ('5% max exception rate')

findings.title: Degenerate count and per-step saturation fraction are computed then dropped — FIX-14 per-(dial,base,step) reporting requirement unmet in the frozen artifact
findings.detail: wc['degenerate'] is accumulated in _collect (lines 426,432) but never written into results — the Phase-B output loops only read wc['skips'] (619-624), so the degenerate fraction per (dial,base,step) is absent from the write-once results JSON. Likewise the per-step saturation fraction is computed in _dial_curves (pts[...][2]) but only the crossing step's value survives as the boolean sat_ok in a candidate; the actual fractions are never emitted. FIX-14 and §11 pre-register: 'report per (dial,base,step) the fraction of the Q=0.85 mass contributed by ≈1.0 saturated readings' and commit it to the hashed artifact. As-is a third party cannot verify from the committed results that a dial's JND was not artifact-driven (only re-derivable by re-parsing every per-asset shard). The eligibility GATE (sat_ok ≤ 0.50) does fire, so the measurement itself is guarded — but the pre-registered reporting on the frozen/hashed file is non-compliant, and this is cheap to fix now versus after the write-once run.
findings.protocol_ref: PROTOCOL §3.1 FIX-14; §11 ('report ... fraction of Q mass ... saturated')

findings.title: FIX-19 extend-finer loop is not executable and Phase-B 'finest' is static — bottomed-out dials can be flagged but not correctly refined
findings.detail: EXTEND_FINER (lines 126-127) and the extra_ratios plumbing (scan_asset arg line 269/299, run_phase_a 'extra' arg line 358) exist, but main() never passes extra to run_phase_a (line 727) and there is no CLI flag to inject finer rungs, so the pre-committed 'prepend finer rungs and re-measure until interior' cannot be run without editing code. Worse, Phase-B computes finest = min(RATIOS)=0.05 / min(ABS_STEPS[kind]) statically (lines 583-585) rather than the minimum step actually present in the tensor; so on any finer re-run (with 0.02/0.03 rungs) at_floor = jnd ≤ 0.05 would keep firing needs_finer for a JND of 0.03 that is genuinely interior to the extended sweep, i.e. the FIX-19 termination is broken. For run-1 (RATIOS only) at_floor is correct, so the first run's non-bottomed dials are fine; but a dial that bottoms out is exactly 'grid too coarse to bound' (coarse = unsafe) and cannot be auto-refined by the harness as pre-registered.
findings.protocol_ref: PROTOCOL §3.1 FIX-19

findings.title: FIX-24 census-balance / survivor-calibration gate not enforced — a JND resting on zero non-survivors is neither excluded nor flagged
findings.detail: Candidate eligibility (line 487) requires only sat_ok and n_valid ≥ M_MIN and boots not None. n_delisted (the count of valid non-survivor assets at the crossing step, line 477) is recorded and emitted in the candidate list but never gates selection nor produces the pre-registered 'survivor-calibrated' flag when a dial/regime JND rests on < M_min non-survivors. FIX-24(a) pre-registers 'valid-asset count ≥ M_min AND census-balanced', and §5 requires flagging any dial/regime whose JND rests on < M_min non-survivors as survivor-calibrated (the FIX-4 late-death sleeve exists specifically to keep this non-zero in Calm-B/COVID). As written, a survivor-only JND passes silently.
findings.protocol_ref: PROTOCOL §5 FIX-24(a); §5 'survivor-calibrated' flag

findings.title: needs_finer inspects only the aggregated sel['jnd'], not each eligible candidate
findings.detail: at_floor/needs_finer (lines 585-587) is computed only from the selected aggregate. FIX-19 says the sweep is too coarse 'if any dial's measured JND lands on the finest rung for ANY base or regime.' While bug #1 is live this is moot (selected == raw finest, so any finest-rung eligible candidate does surface), but once _ci_separated_min is corrected to resolve toward the runner-up, an eligible base/regime that bottoms out at the finest rung while a coarser candidate is selected would no longer be flagged. Check every eligible candidate's jnd ≤ finest, not just the selection.
findings.protocol_ref: PROTOCOL §3.1 FIX-19 ('any base or regime')

findings.title: Window with 1–49 bars is mislabeled 'no_bars_in_window' in the skip census
findings.detail: In scan_asset (lines 311-314) a step whose window slice has 1–49 bars and len(data) < 50 is routed to status 'no_bars_in_window' (the else of the len(data) >= 50 test), even though bars exist — it is really an insufficient-overlap case. Cosmetic only (the reading is correctly skipped), but it pollutes the FIX-13 skip census under a misleading reason key, and 'no_bars_in_window' vs 'overlap_lt_50' are meant to be distinguishable skip reasons.
findings.protocol_ref: PROTOCOL §3.1 FIX-13 (enumerated skip-reason set)

findings.title: temporal_halves uses raw min-across-bases (winner's curse), inconsistent with the CI-separated primary path
findings.detail: halves[hw] = round(min(pts_all), 4) (line 600) takes the raw minimum crossing across bases with no bootstrap separation, unlike the primary estimator. It is report-only (the 'adopt finer if > 1 step apart' is a downstream manual call), so it does not gate N, but a reader comparing temporal_halves to the primary JND should know the two use different (raw vs separated) aggregation — the halves are biased fine relative to the intended estimator.
findings.protocol_ref: PROTOCOL §5 FIX-25

result.summary: Adversarial review of the V3 coverage-calibration engine through the AGGREGATION-EDGE-CASES + RESULTS-SHAPE lens, verified against the code and by isolation tests. One BLOCKER: _ci_separated_min (lines 520-548) always returns the raw finest candidate — in the unseparated branch it falls back to eligible[0] (finest) instead of the pre-registered runner-up, so sel['jnd'] == raw_min in every case and the entire FIX-20/FIX-24 winner's-curse correction is inert (I confirmed with an isolation test: finest=0.08 vs runner-up=0.20 returns 0.08 whether or not the CIs separate). This re-introduces the ~0.85–1.8σ fine-bias over base×regime that the protocol exists to strip, mis-sizes the DSR N toward vacuity, contradicts the documented IMPL choice, and defeats the raw-min-vs-separated-min transparency — and it runs on all 10 tradeable dials before a write-once, hash-frozen 450k-backtest run. Two HIGH gaps are pre-registered guards that are defined-but-unwired: MAX_CRASH_RATE (FIX-13 5% ceiling) is never checked and no per-dial attempt denominator is emitted, and the degenerate count / per-step saturation fraction (FIX-14) are computed then dropped from the frozen results file. Positively verified as CORRECT and NOT findings: the '+1int collides with a ratio step' dedup (base 7→8 and base 15→16 are correctly suppressed; base 35→36 correctly kept); threshold step equal-to-high is kept, exceed dropped; +1int records never leak into JND curves or bootstraps (filtered in _collect, _dial_curves, _bootstrap_jnd); the 'every step ≥ T from the finest rung' case correctly returns steps[0]=finest so needs_finer fires; single-point step lists are guarded everywhere by len<2 checks; and the results JSON is serialization-safe (empirical_confirms values are Python bool, boot_dist np-arrays are stripped before emission, all scalars wrapped). Remaining findings are medium/low (FIX-19 extend-finer loop not executable + static Phase-B 'finest'; FIX-24 census/survivor flag not enforced; needs_finer scope; a mislabeled skip reason; raw-min temporal halves).
